seo.title: Family Therapy for Addiction & Mental Health | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Family-focused therapy for addiction, teen mental health, and co-occurring disorders in St. Louis. We treat the whole family system, not just the individual.

hero.headline: Family Therapy
hero.intro: Addiction and mental health problems don't happen in isolation — they develop within families and affect entire family systems. Family therapy addresses those dynamics directly, rather than treating one person as the patient while everyone else waits on the outside.

whyFamily.heading: Why Family Involvement Matters
paragraphs: Research consistently shows that family involvement improves outcomes for addiction treatment — particularly for teens and young adults. When family members understand the condition, communicate more effectively, and set consistent expectations, recovery is more likely to take hold.

Family therapy is not about assigning blame. It is about understanding how family members have adapted to the problem — sometimes in ways that inadvertently maintain it — and changing those patterns together.
For teens especially, the family is the treatment context. A teenager's recovery depends heavily on what happens at home, not just in the therapist's office.

items.label: Contingency management with family support
items.description: For teens and young adults, family members learn to set meaningful, consistent limits and identify incentives that motivate change — without becoming adversaries.

items.label: Communication and conflict reduction
items.description: Many families in crisis have developed communication patterns — lectures, arguments, silence — that increase rather than decrease tension. We work on changing those patterns.

items.label: Psychoeducation
items.description: Family members often don't understand addiction or mental illness as medical conditions. Understanding the disease model — and what it means for how you respond — changes everything.

items.label: College transition support
items.description: For families navigating a child's transition to college, we facilitate conversations about independence, accountability, and how to stay connected without becoming intrusive.

whoItsFor.heading: Who Family Therapy Is For
items: Families of teenagers dealing with addiction or mental health problems
Parents of college students navigating the transition to independence
Couples where one or both partners are dealing with addiction
Adults whose family relationships have been affected by their own or a loved one's substance use
Family members who want to understand how to help without enabling

faqs.question: What if my family member refuses to participate?
faqs.answer: Family therapy can still be valuable with whoever is willing to come. Often a parent, partner, or sibling can make meaningful changes that shift family dynamics — even without the person with the problem in the room. We also offer consultations for family members on how to approach someone who is refusing help.

faqs.question: Will the therapist take sides?
faqs.answer: No. A family therapist's client is the relationship and the family system, not any one person. The goal is to improve how the family functions together — not to validate any one person's perspective over another's.

faqs.question: Is family therapy the same as couples therapy?
faqs.answer: They overlap but aren't identical. Couples therapy focuses on the relationship between two partners. Family therapy typically involves more family members and may address parent-child relationships, sibling dynamics, and extended family patterns.

faqs.question: How long does family therapy last?
faqs.answer: It depends on the goals and the complexity of the situation. Some families do focused, short-term work — 8–12 sessions — to address a specific crisis. Others benefit from ongoing support as circumstances change.
